# Intent — <Product/Deliverable Name>
<!-- The name should be specific enough to identify this unit of work uniquely.
     Example: "Brushy v1.2" or "HR Policy Document Set — Canada" -->

Owner: <n>
Last Updated: YYYY-MM-DD

---

## Purpose
<!-- What is this and why does it exist?
     2-5 sentences. Focus on the problem being solved and why it matters now.
     This is not a list of features. It is the reason this intent exists.
     If you can't write this clearly, the intent isn't ready. -->

---

## Constraints
<!-- Hard limits the work must operate within. Be specific — vague constraints are ignored constraints.
     Consider:
     - Technical: language, platform, framework, browser, OS
     - Time: deadlines, target dates
     - Budget: cost ceilings, resource limits
     - Policy / Platform: app store rules, legal requirements, brand guidelines
     - External: third-party APIs, systems, or services this work depends on
     If there are no constraints, write: None -->

---

## Dependencies & Files
<!-- Two things live here:
     1. Input files required for this work (icons, copy, specs, datasets, legal text, etc.)
        Lookup order: intents/<this-intent>/files/ → meta/files/
     2. External dependencies — people, systems, approvals, or outputs from other intents
        that this work depends on or assumes are complete.
     If none, write: None -->

---

## Scope (In Scope)
<!-- What is explicitly included in this intent.
     Be specific. If it is not listed here, it may be assumed out of scope.
     Use numbered or bulleted list.
     Think: what will the Manager plan against? -->

---

## Out of Scope (Non-Goals)
<!-- What is explicitly excluded. This is as important as scope.
     If something is obvious but people might assume it's included, list it here.
     Saves rework. Prevents scope creep. -->

---

## Assumptions
<!-- What are you proceeding on without confirmation?
     Assumptions are not ambiguities — they are decisions you've already made
     implicitly. Making them explicit prevents the Manager and Developer from
     making different implicit decisions.
     Example: "Assumes Flutter 3.x is already installed in the build environment."
     Example: "Assumes Canadian employment law applies." -->

---

## Specifics
<!-- The freeform domain detail that makes this intent unique.
     This is where the real work lives: UI layout, data models, workflows,
     business rules, output formats, interaction patterns, content requirements, etc.
     There is no required structure here — use whatever format communicates clearly.
     This section can be short (a paragraph) or long (many subsections).
     The Manager will plan against what is written here.
     If it matters to the outcome, it belongs here. -->

---

## Deliverables
<!-- The specific artifacts, documents, files, or outputs that must exist
     when this intent is complete. Name them specifically.
     Example: "A working Chrome extension zip ready for local installation."
     Example: "Four HR policy documents: Code of Conduct, Leave Policy,
               Termination Policy, Onboarding Checklist."
     If it's not listed here, it is not required. -->

---

## Acceptance Criteria
<!-- How do we know the deliverables are done and correct?
     These are testable, verifiable conditions — not aspirations.
     Write them so the Reviewer can check each one independently.
     Note: Acceptance Criteria defines done. It does not define success.
     Whether this work succeeds in the real world is a human judgment
     that happens after Trail's job is complete. -->

---

## Notes
<!-- Optional. Use this section to record context, rationale, edge cases,
     or anything worth preserving that doesn't fit elsewhere.
     This is not a place for unresolved questions — resolve them before
     the intent is handed to the Manager.
     If none, write: None -->
